title: Equations with Variables on Both Sides

epigraph.text: Come now, and let us reason together, saith the Lord.
epigraph.quote: To solve a problem means to find a way where none is known.

content.text: {To solve a problem means to find a way where none is known.}{George P\'olya}

So far, all our equations have had the variable on only one side. But what 
happens when $x$ appears on *both* sides of the equals sign? The 
equation $5x + 3 = 2x + 15$ has $x$ terms on the left *and* the right. 
How do we proceed?

The answer is simple in concept: gather all the variable terms on one side 
and all the constant terms on the other. The properties of equality allow 
us to do this. Think of it as rearranging the weights on a balance scale 
so that all the "$x$ weights" are on one pan and all the "number 
weights" are on the other.
Gathering Variable Terms on One Side

content.title: Variables on Both Sides
content.content:
When an equation has variables on both sides:

1. **Simplify each side** (distribute, combine like terms).

2. **Move all variable terms to one side** by adding or 
 subtracting.

3. **Move all constant terms to the other side.**

4. **Solve** the resulting equation.

5. **Check** in the original equation.
You may move variables to either side—the solution will be the same. 
Choose whichever makes the arithmetic easier.

content.title: Basic example
content.solution:
We'll move the variable terms to the left side. Subtract $2x$ from both 
sides:
$$
 5x + 3 &= 2x + 15 \\
 5x - 2x + 3 &= 2x - 2x + 15 \\
 3x + 3 &= 15
$$

Now subtract 3 from both sides:
$$
 3x &= 12
$$

Divide by 3:
$$
 x &= 4
$$

**Check:** 
$$
 \text{Left: } 5(4) + 3 &= 20 + 3 = 23 \\
 \text{Right: } 2(4) + 15 &= 8 + 15 = 23 \quad \checkmark
$$

The solution is $x = 4$.

content.title: Moving to the right side
content.solution:
The coefficient of $y$ on the left is 3; on the right, it's 8. To avoid 
negative coefficients, let's move variables to the right side.

Subtract $3y$ from both sides:
$$
 3y - 7 &= 8y + 13 \\
 -7 &= 5y + 13
$$

Subtract 13 from both sides:
$$
 -20 &= 5y
$$

Divide by 5:
$$
 -4 &= y
$$

So $y = -4$.

**Check:**
$$
 \text{Left: } 3(-4) - 7 &= -12 - 7 = -19 \\
 \text{Right: } 8(-4) + 13 &= -32 + 13 = -19 \quad \checkmark
$$

content.title: Simplifying first
content.solution:
First, distribute on both sides:
$$
 4(x + 2) &= 3(x - 5) + x \\
 4x + 8 &= 3x - 15 + x
$$

Combine like terms on the right:
$$
 4x + 8 &= 4x - 15
$$

Subtract $4x$ from both sides:
$$
 8 &= -15
$$

This is false! We have reached a **contradiction**.

The equation has **no solution**. There is no value of $x$ that makes 
the original equation true.

content.text: Special Cases: No Solution and Infinitely Many Solutions
Most equations have exactly one solution. But two special cases can occur.

content.title: Special Cases
content.content:
- **No solution (contradiction):** If solving leads to a false 
 statement like $5 = 3$ or $0 = 7$, the equation has no solution. No 
 value of the variable makes the equation true.

- **Infinitely many solutions (identity):** If solving leads 
 to a true statement like $0 = 0$ or $5 = 5$, the equation is an 
 **identity**—it is true for *all* values of the variable.

content.title: An identity equation
content.solution:
Distribute on the left:
$$
 3(x + 2) &= 3x + 6 \\
 3x + 6 &= 3x + 6
$$

Subtract $3x$ from both sides:
$$
 6 &= 6
$$

This is always true! The equation is an **identity**.

**Solution:** All real numbers. We can write this as 
$\{x \mid x \in \mathbb{R}\}$ or simply "all real numbers."

If you pick any value—say, $x = 10$—and substitute:
$$
 3(10 + 2) &\stackrel{?}{=} 3(10) + 6 \\
 3(12) &= 30 + 6 \\
 36 &= 36 \quad \checkmark
$$

content.title: A contradiction
content.solution:
Distribute:
$$
 2(x + 4) &= 2x + 5 \\
 2x + 8 &= 2x + 5
$$

Subtract $2x$:
$$
 8 &= 5
$$

This is false. No value of $x$ can make $8 = 5$.

**Solution:** No solution. We can write this as $\emptyset$ (the 
empty set) or simply "no solution."

content.title: Recognizing Special Cases
content.content:
Before solving completely, you can sometimes predict the outcome:

- If both sides simplify to expressions with the **same 
 coefficient** on the variable but **different constants**, there 
 is no solution.

- If both sides simplify to **identical expressions**, there 
 are infinitely many solutions.

content.text:
The strategies from Section [sec:multi-step-equations] combine 
naturally with the technique of gathering variables on one side.

content.title: Clearing fractions first
content.solution:
The LCD of 3 and 4 is 12. Multiply both sides by 12:
$$
 12 \cdot \frac{x + 2}{3} &= 12 \cdot \frac{2x - 1}{4} \\
 4(x + 2) &= 3(2x - 1)
$$

Distribute:
$$
 4x + 8 &= 6x - 3
$$

Subtract $4x$:
$$
 8 &= 2x - 3
$$

Add 3:
$$
 11 &= 2x
$$

Divide by 2:
$$
 x &= \frac{11}{2}
$$

**Check:** Substitute $x = \frac{11}{2}$ into the original:

Left: $\dfrac{\frac{11}{2} + 2}{3} = \dfrac{\frac{15}{2}}{3} = \dfrac{15}{6} = \dfrac{5}{2}$

Right: $\dfrac{2 \cdot \frac{11}{2} - 1}{4} = \dfrac{11 - 1}{4} = \dfrac{10}{4} = \dfrac{5}{2}$ $\checkmark$

content.title: Comparing costs
content.problem:
A gym offers two membership plans:

- Plan A: \$50 per month plus \$3 per visit.

- Plan B: \$20 per month plus \$6 per visit.
For how many visits per month would both plans cost the same?
content.solution:
Let $v$ = the number of visits per month.

Cost of Plan A: $50 + 3v$ dollars.
Cost of Plan B: $20 + 6v$ dollars.

Set them equal:
\[
 50 + 3v = 20 + 6v
\]

Subtract $3v$:
$$
 50 &= 20 + 3v
$$

Subtract 20:
$$
 30 &= 3v
$$

Divide by 3:
$$
 v &= 10
$$

At 10 visits per month, both plans cost the same.

**Check:** Plan A: $50 + 3(10) = 50 + 30 = \$80$

Plan B: $20 + 6(10) = 20 + 60 = \$80$ $\checkmark$

**Note:** If you visit fewer than 10 times per month, Plan B is 
cheaper. If you visit more than 10 times, Plan A is cheaper.

content.title: Catch-up problem
content.problem:
Two cars start from the same point. Car A travels at 55 mph; Car B leaves 
2 hours later traveling at 70 mph. How long until Car B catches up to 
Car A?
content.solution:
Let $t$ = time (in hours) that Car B has been traveling.
Then Car A has been traveling for $t + 2$ hours (it left 2 hours earlier).

Distance = rate $\times$ time.

Distance traveled by Car A: $55(t + 2)$ miles.
Distance traveled by Car B: $70t$ miles.

When Car B catches up, both have traveled the same distance:
\[
 55(t + 2) = 70t
\]

Distribute:
$$
 55t + 110 &= 70t
$$

Subtract $55t$:
$$
 110 &= 15t
$$

Divide by 15:
$$
 t &= \frac{110}{15} = \frac{22}{3} \approx 7.33 \text{ hours}
$$

Car B catches up after $7\frac{1}{3}$ hours (7 hours and 20 minutes).

**Check:** Car B travels $70 \times \frac{22}{3} = \frac{1540}{3} \approx 513.3$ miles.

Car A travels $55 \times (\frac{22}{3} + 2) = 55 \times \frac{28}{3} = \frac{1540}{3}$ miles. $\checkmark$

content.title: René Descartes (1596--1650)
content.content:
The French philosopher and mathematician René Descartes established the 
convention of using letters from the end of the alphabet ($x$, $y$, $z$) 
for unknowns and letters from the beginning ($a$, $b$, $c$) for known 
quantities. Before Descartes, different mathematicians used wildly 
different notations. His *La Géométrie* (1637) standardized much 
of the algebraic notation we use today, making it easier for mathematicians 
across the world to communicate their ideas.

marginNotes.text:
Let your eyes look directly forward, 
and your gaze be straight before you. Ponder the path of your feet; then 
all your ways will be sure.

The sum of your word is truth, and every 
one of your righteous rules endures forever.

marginNotes.author: Bertrand Russell
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics, rightly viewed, possesses not 
only truth, but supreme beauty.
